              - text: A TCP implementation SHOULD implement the Nagle algorithm to coalesce short segments (SHLD-7). However, there MUST be a way for an application to disable the Nagle algorithm on an individual connection (MUST-17). In all cases, sending data is also subject to the limitation imposed by the slow start algorithm
              - generic [ref=e1469]:
                - text: "["
                - link "8" [ref=e1470] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc5681
                - text: "]"
              - text: .
              - link "¶" [ref=e1471] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.7.4-3"
            - paragraph [ref=e1472]:
              - text: Since there can be problematic interactions between the Nagle algorithm and delayed acknowledgments, some implementations use minor variations of the Nagle algorithm, such as the one described in
              - link "Appendix A.3" [ref=e1473] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#minshall"
              - text: .
              - link "¶" [ref=e1474] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.7.4-4"
          - generic [ref=e1475]:
            - heading "3.7.5. IPv6 Jumbograms" [level=4] [ref=e1476]:
              - link "3.7.5." [ref=e1477] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.7.5"
              - link "IPv6 Jumbograms" [ref=e1478] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#name-ipv6-jumbograms"
            - paragraph [ref=e1479]:
              - text: In order to support TCP over IPv6 Jumbograms, implementations need to be able to send TCP segments larger than the 64-KB limit that the MSS Option can convey. RFC 2675
              - generic [ref=e1480]:
                - text: "["
                - link "24" [ref=e1481] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc2675
                - text: "]"
              - text: defines that an MSS value of 65,535 bytes is to be treated as infinity, and Path MTU Discovery
              - generic [ref=e1482]:
                - text: "["
                - link "14" [ref=e1483] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8201
                - text: "]"
              - text: is used to determine the actual MSS.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1484] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.7.5-1"
            - paragraph [ref=e1485]:
              - text: The Jumbo Payload Option need not be implemented or understood by IPv6 nodes that do not support attachment to links with an MTU greater than 65,575
              - generic [ref=e1486]:
                - text: "["
                - link "24" [ref=e1487] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc2675
                - text: "]"
              - text: ", and the present IPv6 Node Requirements does not include support for Jumbograms"
              - generic [ref=e1488]:
                - text: "["
                - link "55" [ref=e1489] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8504
                - text: "]"
              - text: .
              - link "¶" [ref=e1490] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.7.5-2"
        - generic [ref=e1492]:
          - heading "3.8. Data Communication" [level=3] [ref=e1493]:
            - link "3.8." [ref=e1494] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#section-3.8"
            - link "Data Communication" [ref=e1495] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#name-data-communication"
          - paragraph [ref=e1496]:
            - text: Once the connection is established, data is communicated by the exchange of segments. Because segments may be lost due to errors (checksum test failure) or network congestion, TCP uses retransmission to ensure delivery of every segment. Duplicate segments may arrive due to network or TCP retransmission. As discussed in the section on sequence numbers (
            - link "Section 3.4" [ref=e1497] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#sequence-numbers"
            - text: ), the TCP implementation performs certain tests on the sequence and acknowledgment numbers in the segments to verify their acceptability.
            - link "¶" [ref=e1498] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#section-3.8-1"
          - paragraph [ref=e1499]:
            - text: The sender of data keeps track of the next sequence number to use in the variable SND.NXT. The receiver of data keeps track of the next sequence number to expect in the variable RCV.NXT. The sender of data keeps track of the oldest unacknowledged sequence number in the variable SND.UNA. If the data flow is momentarily idle and all data sent has been acknowledged, then the three variables will be equal.
            - link "¶" [ref=e1500] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#section-3.8-2"
          - paragraph [ref=e1501]:
            - text: When the sender creates a segment and transmits it, the sender advances SND.NXT. When the receiver accepts a segment, it advances RCV.NXT and sends an acknowledgment. When the data sender receives an acknowledgment, it advances SND.UNA. The extent to which the values of these variables differ is a measure of the delay in the communication. The amount by which the variables are advanced is the length of the data and SYN or FIN flags in the segment. Note that, once in the ESTABLISHED state, all segments must carry current acknowledgment information.
            - link "¶" [ref=e1502] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#section-3.8-3"
          - paragraph [ref=e1503]:
            - text: The CLOSE user call implies a push function (see
            - link "Section 3.9.1" [ref=e1504] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#user-api"
            - text: ), as does the FIN control flag in an incoming segment.
            - link "¶" [ref=e1505] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#section-3.8-4"
          - generic [ref=e1507]:
            - heading "3.8.1. Retransmission Timeout" [level=4] [ref=e1508]:
              - link "3.8.1." [ref=e1509] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.1"
              - link "Retransmission Timeout" [ref=e1510] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#name-retransmission-timeout"
            - paragraph [ref=e1511]:
              - text: Because of the variability of the networks that compose an internetwork system and the wide range of uses of TCP connections, the retransmission timeout (RTO) must be dynamically determined.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1512] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.1-1"
            - paragraph [ref=e1513]:
              - text: The RTO MUST be computed according to the algorithm in
              - generic [ref=e1514]:
                - text: "["
                - link "10" [ref=e1515] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6298
                - text: "]"
              - text: ", including Karn's algorithm for taking RTT samples (MUST-18)."
              - link "¶" [ref=e1516] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.1-2"
            - paragraph [ref=e1517]:
              - text: RFC 793 contains an early example procedure for computing the RTO, based on work mentioned in IEN 177
              - generic [ref=e1518]:
                - text: "["
                - link "71" [ref=e1519] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/ien/ien177.txt
                - text: "]"
              - text: . This was then replaced by the algorithm described in RFC 1122, which was subsequently updated in RFC 2988 and then again in RFC 6298.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1520] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.1-3"
            - paragraph [ref=e1521]:
              - text: RFC 1122 allows that if a retransmitted packet is identical to the original packet (which implies not only that the data boundaries have not changed, but also that none of the headers have changed), then the same IPv4 Identification field MAY be used (see Section
              - link "3.2.1.5" [ref=e1522] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc1122#section-3.2.1.5
              - text: of RFC 1122) (MAY-4). The same IP Identification field may be reused anyways since it is only meaningful when a datagram is fragmented
              - generic [ref=e1523]:
                - text: "["
                - link "44" [ref=e1524] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6864
                - text: "]"
              - text: . TCP implementations should not rely on or typically interact with this IPv4 header field in any way. It is not a reasonable way to indicate duplicate sent segments nor to identify duplicate received segments.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1525] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.1-4"
          - generic [ref=e1526]:
            - heading "3.8.2. TCP Congestion Control" [level=4] [ref=e1527]:
              - link "3.8.2." [ref=e1528] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.2"
              - link "TCP Congestion Control" [ref=e1529] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#name-tcp-congestion-control"
            - paragraph [ref=e1530]:
              - text: RFC 2914
              - generic [ref=e1531]:
                - text: "["
                - link "5" [ref=e1532] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc2914
                - text: "]"
              - text: explains the importance of congestion control for the Internet.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1533] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.2-1"
            - paragraph [ref=e1534]:
              - text: RFC 1122 required implementation of Van Jacobson's congestion control algorithms slow start and congestion avoidance together with exponential backoff for successive RTO values for the same segment. RFC 2581 provided IETF Standards Track description of slow start and congestion avoidance, along with fast retransmit and fast recovery. RFC 5681 is the current description of these algorithms and is the current Standards Track specification providing guidelines for TCP congestion control. RFC 6298 describes exponential backoff of RTO values, including keeping the backed-off value until a subsequent segment with new data has been sent and acknowledged without retransmission.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1535] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.2-2"
            - paragraph [ref=e1536]:
              - text: A TCP endpoint MUST implement the basic congestion control algorithms slow start, congestion avoidance, and exponential backoff of RTO to avoid creating congestion collapse conditions (MUST-19). RFC 5681 and RFC 6298 describe the basic algorithms on the IETF Standards Track that are broadly applicable. Multiple other suitable algorithms exist and have been widely used. Many TCP implementations support a set of alternative algorithms that can be configured for use on the endpoint. An endpoint MAY implement such alternative algorithms provided that the algorithms are conformant with the TCP specifications from the IETF Standards Track as described in RFC 2914, RFC 5033
              - generic [ref=e1537]:
                - text: "["
                - link "7" [ref=e1538] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc5033
                - text: "]"
              - text: ", and RFC 8961"
              - generic [ref=e1539]:
                - text: "["
                - link "15" [ref=e1540] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8961
                - text: "]"
              - text: (MAY-18).
              - link "¶" [ref=e1541] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.2-3"
            - paragraph [ref=e1542]:
              - text: Explicit Congestion Notification (ECN) was defined in RFC 3168 and is an IETF Standards Track enhancement that has many benefits
              - generic [ref=e1543]:
                - text: "["
                - link "51" [ref=e1544] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8087
                - text: "]"
              - text: .
              - link "¶" [ref=e1545] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.2-4"
            - paragraph [ref=e1546]:
              - text: A TCP endpoint SHOULD implement ECN as described in RFC 3168 (SHLD-8).
              - link "¶" [ref=e1547] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.2-5"
          - generic [ref=e1549]:
            - heading "3.8.3. TCP Connection Failures" [level=4] [ref=e1550]:
              - link "3.8.3." [ref=e1551] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.3"
              - link "TCP Connection Failures" [ref=e1552] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#name-tcp-connection-failures"
            - paragraph [ref=e1553]:
              - text: "Excessive retransmission of the same segment by a TCP endpoint indicates some failure of the remote host or the internetwork path. This failure may be of short or long duration. The following procedure MUST be used to handle excessive retransmissions of data segments (MUST-20):"
              - link "¶" [ref=e1554] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.3-1"
            - generic [ref=e1555]:
              - term [ref=e1556]: (a)
              - definition [ref=e1557]:
                - text: There are two thresholds R1 and R2 measuring the amount of retransmission that has occurred for the same segment. R1 and R2 might be measured in time units or as a count of retransmissions (with the current RTO and corresponding backoffs as a conversion factor, if needed).
                - link "¶" [ref=e1558] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.8.3-2.1"
              - term [ref=e1559]: (b)
              - definition [ref=e1560]:
                - text: When the number of transmissions of the same segment reaches or exceeds threshold R1, pass negative advice (see
                - generic [ref=e1561]:
                  - link "Section 3.3.1.4" [ref=e1562] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc1122#section-3.3.1.4
                  - text: of [
                  - link "19" [ref=e1563] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1122
                  - text: "]"
                - text: ) to the IP layer, to trigger dead-gateway diagnosis.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1564] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.8.3-2.2"
              - term [ref=e1565]: (c)
              - definition [ref=e1566]:
                - text: When the number of transmissions of the same segment reaches a threshold R2 greater than R1, close the connection.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1567] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.8.3-2.3"
              - term [ref=e1568]: (d)
              - definition [ref=e1569]:
                - text: An application MUST (MUST-21) be able to set the value for R2 for a particular connection. For example, an interactive application might set R2 to "infinity", giving the user control over when to disconnect.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1570] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.8.3-2.4"
              - term [ref=e1571]: (e)
              - definition [ref=e1572]:
                - text: TCP implementations SHOULD inform the application of the delivery problem (unless such information has been disabled by the application; see the "Asynchronous Reports" section (
                - link "Section 3.9.1.8" [ref=e1573] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#asynchronous-reports"
                - text: )), when R1 is reached and before R2 (SHLD-9). This will allow a remote login application program to inform the user, for example.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1574] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.8.3-2.5"
            - paragraph [ref=e1575]:
              - text: The value of R1 SHOULD correspond to at least 3 retransmissions, at the current RTO (SHLD-10). The value of R2 SHOULD correspond to at least 100 seconds (SHLD-11).
              - link "¶" [ref=e1576] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.3-3"
            - paragraph [ref=e1577]:
              - text: An attempt to open a TCP connection could fail with excessive retransmissions of the SYN segment or by receipt of a RST segment or an ICMP Port Unreachable. SYN retransmissions MUST be handled in the general way just described for data retransmissions, including notification of the application layer.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1578] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.3-4"
            - paragraph [ref=e1579]:
              - text: However, the values of R1 and R2 may be different for SYN and data segments. In particular, R2 for a SYN segment MUST be set large enough to provide retransmission of the segment for at least 3 minutes (MUST-23). The application can close the connection (i.e., give up on the open attempt) sooner, of course.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1580] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.3-5"
          - generic [ref=e1581]:
            - heading "3.8.4. TCP Keep-Alives" [level=4] [ref=e1582]:
              - link "3.8.4." [ref=e1583] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.4"
              - link "TCP Keep-Alives" [ref=e1584] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#name-tcp-keep-alives"
            - paragraph [ref=e1585]:
              - text: A TCP connection is said to be "idle" if for some long amount of time there have been no incoming segments received and there is no new or unacknowledged data to be sent.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1586] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.4-1"
            - paragraph [ref=e1587]:
              - text: Implementers MAY include "keep-alives" in their TCP implementations (MAY-5), although this practice is not universally accepted. Some TCP implementations, however, have included a keep-alive mechanism. To confirm that an idle connection is still active, these implementations send a probe segment designed to elicit a response from the TCP peer. Such a segment generally contains SEG.SEQ = SND.NXT-1 and may or may not contain one garbage octet of data. If keep-alives are included, the application MUST be able to turn them on or off for each TCP connection (MUST-24), and they MUST default to off (MUST-25).
              - link "¶" [ref=e1588] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.4-2"
            - paragraph [ref=e1589]:
              - text: Keep-alive packets MUST only be sent when no sent data is outstanding, and no data or acknowledgment packets have been received for the connection within an interval (MUST-26). This interval MUST be configurable (MUST-27) and MUST default to no less than two hours (MUST-28).
              - link "¶" [ref=e1590] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.4-3"
            - paragraph [ref=e1591]:
              - text: It is extremely important to remember that ACK segments that contain no data are not reliably transmitted by TCP. Consequently, if a keep-alive mechanism is implemented it MUST NOT interpret failure to respond to any specific probe as a dead connection (MUST-29).
              - link "¶" [ref=e1592] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.4-4"
            - paragraph [ref=e1593]:
              - text: An implementation SHOULD send a keep-alive segment with no data (SHLD-12); however, it MAY be configurable to send a keep-alive segment containing one garbage octet (MAY-6), for compatibility with erroneous TCP implementations.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1594] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.4-5"
          - generic [ref=e1596]:
            - heading "3.8.5. The Communication of Urgent Information" [level=4] [ref=e1597]:
              - link "3.8.5." [ref=e1598] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.5"
              - link "The Communication of Urgent Information" [ref=e1599] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#name-the-communication-of-urgent"
            - paragraph [ref=e1600]:
              - text: As a result of implementation differences and middlebox interactions, new applications SHOULD NOT employ the TCP urgent mechanism (SHLD-13). However, TCP implementations MUST still include support for the urgent mechanism (MUST-30). Information on how some TCP implementations interpret the urgent pointer can be found in RFC 6093
              - generic [ref=e1601]:
                - text: "["
                - link "39" [ref=e1602] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6093
                - text: "]"
              - text: .
              - link "¶" [ref=e1603] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.5-1"
            - paragraph [ref=e1604]:
              - text: The objective of the TCP urgent mechanism is to allow the sending user to stimulate the receiving user to accept some urgent data and to permit the receiving TCP endpoint to indicate to the receiving user when all the currently known urgent data has been received by the user.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1605] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.5-2"
            - paragraph [ref=e1606]:
              - text: This mechanism permits a point in the data stream to be designated as the end of urgent information. Whenever this point is in advance of the receive sequence number (RCV.NXT) at the receiving TCP endpoint, then the TCP implementation must tell the user to go into "urgent mode"; when the receive sequence number catches up to the urgent pointer, the TCP implementation must tell user to go into "normal mode". If the urgent pointer is updated while the user is in "urgent mode", the update will be invisible to the user.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1607] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.5-3"
            - paragraph [ref=e1608]:
              - text: The method employs an urgent field that is carried in all segments transmitted. The URG control flag indicates that the urgent field is meaningful and must be added to the segment sequence number to yield the urgent pointer. The absence of this flag indicates that there is no urgent data outstanding.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1609] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.5-4"
            - paragraph [ref=e1610]:
              - text: To send an urgent indication, the user must also send at least one data octet. If the sending user also indicates a push, timely delivery of the urgent information to the destination process is enhanced. Note that because changes in the urgent pointer correspond to data being written by a sending application, the urgent pointer cannot "recede" in the sequence space, but a TCP receiver should be robust to invalid urgent pointer values.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1611] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.5-5"
            - paragraph [ref=e1612]:
              - text: A TCP implementation MUST support a sequence of urgent data of any length (MUST-31)
              - generic [ref=e1613]:
                - text: "["
                - link "19" [ref=e1614] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1122
                - text: "]"
              - text: .
              - link "¶" [ref=e1615] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.5-6"
            - paragraph [ref=e1616]:
              - text: The urgent pointer MUST point to the sequence number of the octet following the urgent data (MUST-62).
              - link "¶" [ref=e1617] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.5-7"
            - paragraph [ref=e1618]:
              - text: A TCP implementation MUST (MUST-32) inform the application layer asynchronously whenever it receives an urgent pointer and there was previously no pending urgent data, or whenever the urgent pointer advances in the data stream. The TCP implementation MUST (MUST-33) provide a way for the application to learn how much urgent data remains to be read from the connection, or at least to determine whether more urgent data remains to be read
              - generic [ref=e1619]:
                - text: "["
                - link "19" [ref=e1620] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1122
                - text: "]"
              - text: .
              - link "¶" [ref=e1621] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.5-8"
          - generic [ref=e1622]:
            - heading "3.8.6. Managing the Window" [level=4] [ref=e1623]:
              - link "3.8.6." [ref=e1624] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.6"
              - link "Managing the Window" [ref=e1625] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#name-managing-the-window"
            - paragraph [ref=e1626]:
              - text: The window sent in each segment indicates the range of sequence numbers the sender of the window (the data receiver) is currently prepared to accept. There is an assumption that this is related to the data buffer space currently available for this connection.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1627] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.6-1"
            - paragraph [ref=e1628]:
              - text: The sending TCP endpoint packages the data to be transmitted into segments that fit the current window, and may repackage segments on the retransmission queue. Such repackaging is not required but may be helpful.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1629] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.6-2"
            - paragraph [ref=e1630]:
              - text: In a connection with a one-way data flow, the window information will be carried in acknowledgment segments that all have the same sequence number, so there will be no way to reorder them if they arrive out of order. This is not a serious problem, but it will allow the window information to be on occasion temporarily based on old reports from the data receiver. A refinement to avoid this problem is to act on the window information from segments that carry the highest acknowledgment number (that is, segments with an acknowledgment number equal to or greater than the highest previously received).
              - link "¶" [ref=e1631] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.6-3"
            - paragraph [ref=e1632]:
              - text: Indicating a large window encourages transmissions. If more data arrives than can be accepted, it will be discarded. This will result in excessive retransmissions, adding unnecessarily to the load on the network and the TCP endpoints. Indicating a small window may restrict the transmission of data to the point of introducing a round-trip delay between each new segment transmitted.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1633] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.6-4"
            - paragraph [ref=e1634]:
              - text: The mechanisms provided allow a TCP endpoint to advertise a large window and to subsequently advertise a much smaller window without having accepted that much data. This so-called "shrinking the window" is strongly discouraged. The robustness principle
              - generic [ref=e1635]:
                - text: "["
                - link "19" [ref=e1636] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1122
                - text: "]"
              - text: dictates that TCP peers will not shrink the window themselves, but will be prepared for such behavior on the part of other TCP peers.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1637] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.6-5"
            - paragraph [ref=e1638]:
              - text: A TCP receiver SHOULD NOT shrink the window, i.e., move the right window edge to the left (SHLD-14). However, a sending TCP peer MUST be robust against window shrinking, which may cause the "usable window" (see
              - link "Section 3.8.6.2.1" [ref=e1639] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#SWSsender"
              - text: ) to become negative (MUST-34).
              - link "¶" [ref=e1640] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.6-6"
            - paragraph [ref=e1641]:
              - text: If this happens, the sender SHOULD NOT send new data (SHLD-15), but SHOULD retransmit normally the old unacknowledged data between SND.UNA and SND.UNA+SND.WND (SHLD-16). The sender MAY also retransmit old data beyond SND.UNA+SND.WND (MAY-7), but SHOULD NOT time out the connection if data beyond the right window edge is not acknowledged (SHLD-17). If the window shrinks to zero, the TCP implementation MUST probe it in the standard way (described below) (MUST-35).
              - link "¶" [ref=e1642] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.8.6-7"
            - generic [ref=e1644]:
              - heading "3.8.6.1. Zero-Window Probing" [level=5] [ref=e1645]:
                - link "3.8.6.1." [ref=e1646] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.8.6.1"
                - link "Zero-Window Probing" [ref=e1647] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#name-zero-window-probing"
              - paragraph [ref=e1648]:
                - text: The sending TCP peer must regularly transmit at least one octet of new data (if available), or retransmit to the receiving TCP peer even if the send window is zero, in order to "probe" the window. This retransmission is essential to guarantee that when either TCP peer has a zero window the reopening of the window will be reliably reported to the other. This is referred to as Zero-Window Probing (ZWP) in other documents.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1649] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.8.6.1-1"
              - paragraph [ref=e1650]:
                - text: Probing of zero (offered) windows MUST be supported (MUST-36).
                - link "¶" [ref=e1651] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.8.6.1-2"
              - paragraph [ref=e1652]:
                - text: A TCP implementation MAY keep its offered receive window closed indefinitely (MAY-8). As long as the receiving TCP peer continues to send acknowledgments in response to the probe segments, the sending TCP peer MUST allow the connection to stay open (MUST-37). This enables TCP to function in scenarios such as the "printer ran out of paper" situation described in
                - generic [ref=e1653]:
                  - link "Section 4.2.2.17" [ref=e1654] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc1122#section-4.2.2.17
                  - text: of [
                  - link "19" [ref=e1655] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1122
                  - text: "]"
                - text: . The behavior is subject to the implementation's resource management concerns, as noted in
                - generic [ref=e1656]:
                  - text: "["
                  - link "41" [ref=e1657] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6429
                  - text: "]"
                - text: .
                - link "¶" [ref=e1658] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.8.6.1-3"
              - paragraph [ref=e1659]:
                - text: When the receiving TCP peer has a zero window and a segment arrives, it must still send an acknowledgment showing its next expected sequence number and current window (zero).
                - link "¶" [ref=e1660] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.8.6.1-4"
              - paragraph [ref=e1661]:
                - text: The transmitting host SHOULD send the first zero-window probe when a zero window has existed for the retransmission timeout period (SHLD-29) (
                - link "Section 3.8.1" [ref=e1662] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#RTO"
                - text: ), and SHOULD increase exponentially the interval between successive probes (SHLD-30).
                - link "¶" [ref=e1663] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.8.6.1-5"
            - generic [ref=e1664]:
              - heading "3.8.6.2. Silly Window Syndrome Avoidance" [level=5] [ref=e1665]:
                - link "3.8.6.2." [ref=e1666] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2"
                - link "Silly Window Syndrome Avoidance" [ref=e1667] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#name-silly-window-syndrome-avoid"
              - paragraph [ref=e1668]:
                - text: The "Silly Window Syndrome" (SWS) is a stable pattern of small incremental window movements resulting in extremely poor TCP performance. Algorithms to avoid SWS are described below for both the sending side and the receiving side. RFC 1122 contains more detailed discussion of the SWS problem. Note that the Nagle algorithm and the sender SWS avoidance algorithm play complementary roles in improving performance. The Nagle algorithm discourages sending tiny segments when the data to be sent increases in small increments, while the SWS avoidance algorithm discourages small segments resulting from the right window edge advancing in small increments.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1669] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2-1"
              - generic [ref=e1671]:
                - heading "3.8.6.2.1. Sender's Algorithm -- When to Send Data" [level=6] [ref=e1672]:
                  - link "3.8.6.2.1." [ref=e1673] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.1"
                  - link "Sender's Algorithm -- When to Send Data" [ref=e1674] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#name-senders-algorithm-when-to-s"
                - paragraph [ref=e1675]:
                  - text: A TCP implementation MUST include a SWS avoidance algorithm in the sender (MUST-38).
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1676] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.1-1"
                - paragraph [ref=e1677]:
                  - text: The Nagle algorithm from
                  - link "Section 3.7.4" [ref=e1678] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#nagle"
                  - text: additionally describes how to coalesce short segments.
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1679] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.1-2"
                - paragraph [ref=e1680]:
                  - text: The sender's SWS avoidance algorithm is more difficult than the receiver's because the sender does not know (directly) the receiver's total buffer space (RCV.BUFF). An approach that has been found to work well is for the sender to calculate Max(SND.WND), which is the maximum send window it has seen so far on the connection, and to use this value as an estimate of RCV.BUFF. Unfortunately, this can only be an estimate; the receiver may at any time reduce the size of RCV.BUFF. To avoid a resulting deadlock, it is necessary to have a timeout to force transmission of data, overriding the SWS avoidance algorithm. In practice, this timeout should seldom occur.
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1681] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.1-3"
                - paragraph [ref=e1682]:
                  - text: "The \"usable window\" is:"
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1683] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.1-4"
                - paragraph [ref=e1684]:
                  - text: U = SND.UNA + SND.WND - SND.NXT
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1685] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.1-5"
                - paragraph [ref=e1686]:
                  - text: i.e., the offered window less the amount of data sent but not acknowledged. If D is the amount of data queued in the sending TCP endpoint but not yet sent, then the following set of rules is recommended.
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1687] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.1-6"
                - paragraph [ref=e1688]:
                  - text: "Send data:"
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1689] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.1-7"
                - generic [ref=e1690]:
                  - term [ref=e1691]: (1)
                  - definition [ref=e1692]:
                    - paragraph [ref=e1693]:
                      - text: "if a maximum-sized segment can be sent, i.e., if:"
                      - link "¶" [ref=e1694] [cursor=pointer]:
                        - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.1-8.1.1"
                    - paragraph [ref=e1695]:
                      - text: min(D,U) >= Eff.snd.MSS;
                      - link "¶" [ref=e1696] [cursor=pointer]:
                        - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.1-8.1.2"
                  - term [ref=e1697]: (2)
                  - definition [ref=e1698]:
                    - paragraph [ref=e1699]:
                      - text: "or if the data is pushed and all queued data can be sent now, i.e., if:"
                      - link "¶" [ref=e1700] [cursor=pointer]:
                        - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.1-8.2.1"
                    - paragraph [ref=e1701]:
                      - text: "[SND.NXT = SND.UNA and] PUSHed and D <= U"
                      - link "¶" [ref=e1702] [cursor=pointer]:
                        - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.1-8.2.2"
                    - paragraph [ref=e1703]:
                      - text: (the bracketed condition is imposed by the Nagle algorithm);
                      - link "¶" [ref=e1704] [cursor=pointer]:
                        - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.1-8.2.3"
                  - term [ref=e1705]: (3)
                  - definition [ref=e1706]:
                    - paragraph [ref=e1707]:
                      - text: "or if at least a fraction Fs of the maximum window can be sent, i.e., if:"
                      - link "¶" [ref=e1708] [cursor=pointer]:
                        - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.1-8.3.1"
                    - paragraph [ref=e1709]:
                      - text: "[SND.NXT = SND.UNA and]"
                      - link "¶" [ref=e1710] [cursor=pointer]:
                        - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.1-8.3.2"
                    - paragraph [ref=e1711]:
                      - text: min(D,U) >= Fs * Max(SND.WND);
                      - link "¶" [ref=e1712] [cursor=pointer]:
                        - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.1-8.3.3"
                  - term [ref=e1713]: (4)
                  - definition [ref=e1714]:
                    - text: or if the override timeout occurs.
                    - link "¶" [ref=e1715] [cursor=pointer]:
                      - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.1-8.4"
                - paragraph [ref=e1716]:
                  - text: Here Fs is a fraction whose recommended value is 1/2. The override timeout should be in the range 0.1 - 1.0 seconds. It may be convenient to combine this timer with the timer used to probe zero windows (
                  - link "Section 3.8.6.1" [ref=e1717] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#zwp"
                  - text: ).
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1718] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.1-9"
              - generic [ref=e1719]:
                - heading "3.8.6.2.2. Receiver's Algorithm -- When to Send a Window Update" [level=6] [ref=e1720]:
                  - link "3.8.6.2.2." [ref=e1721] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.2"
                  - link "Receiver's Algorithm -- When to Send a Window Update" [ref=e1722] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#name-receivers-algorithm-when-to"
                - paragraph [ref=e1723]:
                  - text: A TCP implementation MUST include a SWS avoidance algorithm in the receiver (MUST-39).
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1724] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.2-1"
                - paragraph [ref=e1725]:
                  - text: The receiver's SWS avoidance algorithm determines when the right window edge may be advanced; this is customarily known as "updating the window". This algorithm combines with the delayed ACK algorithm (
                  - link "Section 3.8.6.3" [ref=e1726] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#delACK"
                  - text: ) to determine when an ACK segment containing the current window will really be sent to the receiver.
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1727] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.2-2"
                - paragraph [ref=e1728]:
                  - text: The solution to receiver SWS is to avoid advancing the right window edge RCV.NXT+RCV.WND in small increments, even if data is received from the network in small segments.
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1729] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.2-3"
                - paragraph [ref=e1730]:
                  - text: Suppose the total receive buffer space is RCV.BUFF. At any given moment, RCV.USER octets of this total may be tied up with data that has been received and acknowledged but that the user process has not yet consumed. When the connection is quiescent, RCV.WND = RCV.BUFF and RCV.USER = 0.
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1731] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.2-4"
                - paragraph [ref=e1732]:
                  - text: "Keeping the right window edge fixed as data arrives and is acknowledged requires that the receiver offer less than its full buffer space, i.e., the receiver must specify a RCV.WND that keeps RCV.NXT+RCV.WND constant as RCV.NXT increases. Thus, the total buffer space RCV.BUFF is generally divided into three parts:"
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1733] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.2-5"
                - generic [ref=e1734]:
                  - generic [ref=e1735]: "|<------- RCV.BUFF ---------------->| 1 2 3 ----|---------|------------------|------|---- RCV.NXT ^ (Fixed) 1 - RCV.USER = data received but not yet consumed; 2 - RCV.WND = space advertised to sender; 3 - Reduction = space available but not yet advertised."
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1736] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.2-6"
                - paragraph [ref=e1737]:
                  - text: "The suggested SWS avoidance algorithm for the receiver is to keep RCV.NXT+RCV.WND fixed until the reduction satisfies:"
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1738] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.2-7"
                - generic [ref=e1739]:
                  - generic [ref=e1740]: RCV.BUFF - RCV.USER - RCV.WND >= min( Fr * RCV.BUFF, Eff.snd.MSS )
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1741] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.2-8"
                - paragraph [ref=e1742]:
                  - text: where Fr is a fraction whose recommended value is 1/2, and Eff.snd.MSS is the effective send MSS for the connection (see
                  - link "Section 3.7.1" [ref=e1743] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#mss"
                  - text: ). When the inequality is satisfied, RCV.WND is set to RCV.BUFF-RCV.USER.
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1744] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.2-9"
                - paragraph [ref=e1745]:
                  - text: "Note that the general effect of this algorithm is to advance RCV.WND in increments of Eff.snd.MSS (for realistic receive buffers: Eff.snd.MSS < RCV.BUFF/2). Note also that the receiver must use its own Eff.snd.MSS, making the assumption that it is the same as the sender's."
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1746] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.8.6.2.2-10"
            - generic [ref=e1748]:
              - heading "3.8.6.3. Delayed Acknowledgments -- When to Send an ACK Segment" [level=5] [ref=e1749]:
                - link "3.8.6.3." [ref=e1750] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.8.6.3"
                - link "Delayed Acknowledgments -- When to Send an ACK Segment" [ref=e1751] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#name-delayed-acknowledgments-whe"
              - paragraph [ref=e1752]:
                - text: A host that is receiving a stream of TCP data segments can increase efficiency in both the network and the hosts by sending fewer than one ACK (acknowledgment) segment per data segment received; this is known as a "delayed ACK".
                - link "¶" [ref=e1753] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.8.6.3-1"
              - paragraph [ref=e1754]:
                - text: A TCP endpoint SHOULD implement a delayed ACK (SHLD-18), but an ACK should not be excessively delayed; in particular, the delay MUST be less than 0.5 seconds (MUST-40). An ACK SHOULD be generated for at least every second full-sized segment or 2*RMSS bytes of new data (where RMSS is the MSS specified by the TCP endpoint receiving the segments to be acknowledged, or the default value if not specified) (SHLD-19). Excessive delays on ACKs can disturb the round-trip timing and packet "clocking" algorithms. More complete discussion of delayed ACK behavior is in Section
                - link "4.2" [ref=e1755] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5681#section-4.2
                - text: of RFC 5681
                - generic [ref=e1756]:
                  - text: "["
                  - link "8" [ref=e1757] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc5681
                  - text: "]"
                - text: ", including recommendations to immediately acknowledge out-of-order segments, segments above a gap in sequence space, or segments that fill all or part of a gap, in order to accelerate loss recovery."
                - link "¶" [ref=e1758] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.8.6.3-2"
              - paragraph [ref=e1759]:
                - text: Note that there are several current practices that further lead to a reduced number of ACKs, including generic receive offload (GRO)
                - generic [ref=e1760]:
                  - text: "["
                  - link "72" [ref=e1761] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: https://www.kernel.org/doc/html/latest/networking/segmentation-offloads.html
                  - text: "]"
                - text: ", ACK compression, and ACK decimation"
                - generic [ref=e1762]:
                  - text: "["
                  - link "28" [ref=e1763] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc3449
                  - text: "]"
                - text: .
                - link "¶" [ref=e1764] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.8.6.3-3"
        - generic [ref=e1765]:
          - heading "3.9. Interfaces" [level=3] [ref=e1766]:
            - link "3.9." [ref=e1767] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#section-3.9"
            - link "Interfaces" [ref=e1768] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#name-interfaces"
          - paragraph [ref=e1769]:
            - text: "There are of course two interfaces of concern: the user/TCP interface and the TCP/lower-level interface. We have a fairly elaborate model of the user/TCP interface, but the interface to the lower-level protocol module is left unspecified here since it will be specified in detail by the specification of the lower-level protocol. For the case that the lower level is IP, we note some of the parameter values that TCP implementations might use."
            - link "¶" [ref=e1770] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#section-3.9-1"
          - generic [ref=e1772]:
            - heading "3.9.1. User/TCP Interface" [level=4] [ref=e1773]:
              - link "3.9.1." [ref=e1774] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.9.1"
              - link "User/TCP Interface" [ref=e1775] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#name-user-tcp-interface"
            - paragraph [ref=e1776]:
              - text: The following functional description of user commands to the TCP implementation is, at best, fictional, since every operating system will have different facilities. Consequently, we must warn readers that different TCP implementations may have different user interfaces. However, all TCP implementations must provide a certain minimum set of services to guarantee that all TCP implementations can support the same protocol hierarchy. This section specifies the functional interfaces required of all TCP implementations.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1777] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.9.1-1"
            - paragraph [ref=e1778]:
              - generic [ref=e1779]:
                - link "Section 3.1" [ref=e1780] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8303#section-3.1
                - text: of [
                - link "53" [ref=e1781] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8303
                - text: "]"
              - text: also identifies primitives provided by TCP and could be used as an additional reference for implementers.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1782] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.9.1-2"
            - paragraph [ref=e1783]:
              - text: The following sections functionally characterize a user/TCP interface. The notation used is similar to most procedure or function calls in high-level languages, but this usage is not meant to rule out trap-type service calls.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1784] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.9.1-3"
            - paragraph [ref=e1785]:
              - text: The user commands described below specify the basic functions the TCP implementation must perform to support interprocess communication. Individual implementations must define their own exact format and may provide combinations or subsets of the basic functions in single calls. In particular, some implementations may wish to automatically OPEN a connection on the first SEND or RECEIVE issued by the user for a given connection.
              - link "¶" [ref=e1786] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.9.1-4"
            - paragraph [ref=e1787]:
              - text: "In providing interprocess communication facilities, the TCP implementation must not only accept commands, but must also return information to the processes it serves. The latter consists of:"
              - link "¶" [ref=e1788] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#section-3.9.1-5"
            - generic [ref=e1789]:
              - term [ref=e1790]: (a)
              - definition [ref=e1791]:
                - text: general information about a connection (e.g., interrupts, remote close, binding of unspecified remote socket).
                - link "¶" [ref=e1792] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1-6.1"
              - term [ref=e1793]: (b)
              - definition [ref=e1794]:
                - text: replies to specific user commands indicating success or various types of failure.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1795] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1-6.2"
            - generic [ref=e1796]:
              - heading "3.9.1.1. Open" [level=5] [ref=e1797]:
                - link "3.9.1.1." [ref=e1798] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.1"
                - link "Open" [ref=e1799] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#name-open"
              - paragraph [ref=e1800]:
                - text: "Format: OPEN (local port, remote socket, active/passive [, timeout] [, Diffserv field] [, security/compartment] [, local IP address] [, options]) -> local connection name"
                - link "¶" [ref=e1801] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.1-1"
              - paragraph [ref=e1802]:
                - text: If the active/passive flag is set to passive, then this is a call to LISTEN for an incoming connection. A passive OPEN may have either a fully specified remote socket to wait for a particular connection or an unspecified remote socket to wait for any call. A fully specified passive call can be made active by the subsequent execution of a SEND.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1803] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.1-2"
              - paragraph [ref=e1804]:
                - text: A transmission control block (TCB) is created and partially filled in with data from the OPEN command parameters.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1805] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.1-3"
              - paragraph [ref=e1806]:
                - text: Every passive OPEN call either creates a new connection record in LISTEN state, or it returns an error; it MUST NOT affect any previously created connection record (MUST-41).
                - link "¶" [ref=e1807] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.1-4"
              - paragraph [ref=e1808]:
                - text: A TCP implementation that supports multiple concurrent connections MUST provide an OPEN call that will functionally allow an application to LISTEN on a port while a connection block with the same local port is in SYN-SENT or SYN-RECEIVED state (MUST-42).
                - link "¶" [ref=e1809] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.1-5"
              - paragraph [ref=e1810]:
                - text: On an active OPEN command, the TCP endpoint will begin the procedure to synchronize (i.e., establish) the connection at once.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1811] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.1-6"
              - paragraph [ref=e1812]:
                - text: The timeout, if present, permits the caller to set up a timeout for all data submitted to TCP. If data is not successfully delivered to the destination within the timeout period, the TCP endpoint will abort the connection. The present global default is five minutes.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1813] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.1-7"
              - paragraph [ref=e1814]:
                - text: The TCP implementation or some component of the operating system will verify the user's authority to open a connection with the specified Diffserv field value or security/compartment. The absence of a Diffserv field value or security/compartment specification in the OPEN call indicates the default values must be used.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1815] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.1-8"
              - paragraph [ref=e1816]:
                - text: TCP will accept incoming requests as matching only if the security/compartment information is exactly the same as that requested in the OPEN call.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1817] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.1-9"
              - paragraph [ref=e1818]:
                - text: The Diffserv field value indicated by the user only impacts outgoing packets, may be altered en route through the network, and has no direct bearing or relation to received packets.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1819] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.1-10"
              - paragraph [ref=e1820]:
                - text: A local connection name will be returned to the user by the TCP implementation. The local connection name can then be used as a shorthand term for the connection defined by the <local socket, remote socket> pair.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1821] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.1-11"
              - paragraph [ref=e1822]:
                - text: The optional "local IP address" parameter MUST be supported to allow the specification of the local IP address (MUST-43). This enables applications that need to select the local IP address used when multihoming is present.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1823] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.1-12"
              - paragraph [ref=e1824]:
                - text: A passive OPEN call with a specified "local IP address" parameter will await an incoming connection request to that address. If the parameter is unspecified, a passive OPEN will await an incoming connection request to any local IP address and then bind the local IP address of the connection to the particular address that is used.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1825] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.1-13"
              - paragraph [ref=e1826]:
                - text: For an active OPEN call, a specified "local IP address" parameter will be used for opening the connection. If the parameter is unspecified, the host will choose an appropriate local IP address (see RFC {{REDACTED_ADDRESS}}, Section
                - link "3.3.4.2" [ref=e1827] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc1122#section-3.3.4.2
                - text: ).
                - link "¶" [ref=e1828] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.1-14"
              - paragraph [ref=e1829]:
                - text: If an application on a multihomed host does not specify the local IP address when actively opening a TCP connection, then the TCP implementation MUST ask the IP layer to select a local IP address before sending the (first) SYN (MUST-44). See the function GET_SRCADDR() in Section
                - link "3.4" [ref=e1830] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc1122#section-3.4
                - text: of RFC 1122.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1831] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.1-15"
              - paragraph [ref=e1832]:
                - text: At all other times, a previous segment has either been sent or received on this connection, and TCP implementations MUST use the same local address that was used in those previous segments (MUST-45).
                - link "¶" [ref=e1833] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.1-16"
              - paragraph [ref=e1834]:
                - text: A TCP implementation MUST reject as an error a local OPEN call for an invalid remote IP address (e.g., a broadcast or multicast address) (MUST-46).
                - link "¶" [ref=e1835] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.1-17"
            - generic [ref=e1836]:
              - heading "3.9.1.2. Send" [level=5] [ref=e1837]:
                - link "3.9.1.2." [ref=e1838] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.2"
                - link "Send" [ref=e1839] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#name-send"
              - paragraph [ref=e1840]:
                - text: "Format: SEND (local connection name, buffer address, byte count, URGENT flag [, PUSH flag] [, timeout])"
                - link "¶" [ref=e1841] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.2-1"
              - paragraph [ref=e1842]:
                - text: This call causes the data contained in the indicated user buffer to be sent on the indicated connection. If the connection has not been opened, the SEND is considered an error. Some implementations may allow users to SEND first; in which case, an automatic OPEN would be done. For example, this might be one way for application data to be included in SYN segments. If the calling process is not authorized to use this connection, an error is returned.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1843] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.2-2"
              - paragraph [ref=e1844]:
                - text: "A TCP endpoint MAY implement PUSH flags on SEND calls (MAY-15). If PUSH flags are not implemented, then the sending TCP peer: (1) MUST NOT buffer data indefinitely (MUST-60), and (2) MUST set the PSH bit in the last buffered segment (i.e., when there is no more queued data to be sent) (MUST-61). The remaining description below assumes the PUSH flag is supported on SEND calls."
                - link "¶" [ref=e1845] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.2-3"
              - paragraph [ref=e1846]:
                - text: If the PUSH flag is set, the application intends the data to be transmitted promptly to the receiver, and the PSH bit will be set in the last TCP segment created from the buffer.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1847] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.2-4"
              - paragraph [ref=e1848]:
                - text: The PSH bit is not a record marker and is independent of segment boundaries. The transmitter SHOULD collapse successive bits when it packetizes data, to send the largest possible segment (SHLD-27).
                - link "¶" [ref=e1849] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.2-5"
              - paragraph [ref=e1850]:
                - text: If the PUSH flag is not set, the data may be combined with data from subsequent SENDs for transmission efficiency. When an application issues a series of SEND calls without setting the PUSH flag, the TCP implementation MAY aggregate the data internally without sending it (MAY-16). Note that when the Nagle algorithm is in use, TCP implementations may buffer the data before sending, without regard to the PUSH flag (see
                - link "Section 3.7.4" [ref=e1851] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#nagle"
                - text: ).
                - link "¶" [ref=e1852] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.2-6"
              - paragraph [ref=e1853]:
                - text: An application program is logically required to set the PUSH flag in a SEND call whenever it needs to force delivery of the data to avoid a communication deadlock. However, a TCP implementation SHOULD send a maximum-sized segment whenever possible (SHLD-28) to improve performance (see
                - link "Section 3.8.6.2.1" [ref=e1854] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#SWSsender"
                - text: ).
                - link "¶" [ref=e1855] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.2-7"
              - paragraph [ref=e1856]:
                - text: New applications SHOULD NOT set the URGENT flag
                - generic [ref=e1857]:
                  - text: "["
                  - link "39" [ref=e1858] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6093
                  - text: "]"
                - text: due to implementation differences and middlebox issues (SHLD-13).
                - link "¶" [ref=e1859] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.2-8"
              - paragraph [ref=e1860]:
                - text: If the URGENT flag is set, segments sent to the destination TCP peer will have the urgent pointer set. The receiving TCP peer will signal the urgent condition to the receiving process if the urgent pointer indicates that data preceding the urgent pointer has not been consumed by the receiving process. The purpose of the URGENT flag is to stimulate the receiver to process the urgent data and to indicate to the receiver when all the currently known urgent data has been received. The number of times the sending user's TCP implementation signals urgent will not necessarily be equal to the number of times the receiving user will be notified of the presence of urgent data.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1861] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.2-9"
              - paragraph [ref=e1862]:
                - text: If no remote socket was specified in the OPEN, but the connection is established (e.g., because a LISTENing connection has become specific due to a remote segment arriving for the local socket), then the designated buffer is sent to the implied remote socket. Users who make use of OPEN with an unspecified remote socket can make use of SEND without ever explicitly knowing the remote socket address.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1863] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.2-10"
              - paragraph [ref=e1864]:
                - text: However, if a SEND is attempted before the remote socket becomes specified, an error will be returned. Users can use the STATUS call to determine the status of the connection. Some TCP implementations may notify the user when an unspecified socket is bound.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1865] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.2-11"
              - paragraph [ref=e1866]:
                - text: If a timeout is specified, the current user timeout for this connection is changed to the new one.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1867] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.2-12"
              - paragraph [ref=e1868]:
                - text: In the simplest implementation, SEND would not return control to the sending process until either the transmission was complete or the timeout had been exceeded. However, this simple method is both subject to deadlocks (for example, both sides of the connection might try to do SENDs before doing any RECEIVEs) and offers poor performance, so it is not recommended. A more sophisticated implementation would return immediately to allow the process to run concurrently with network I/O, and, furthermore, to allow multiple SENDs to be in progress. Multiple SENDs are served in first come, first served order, so the TCP endpoint will queue those it cannot service immediately.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1869] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.2-13"
              - paragraph [ref=e1870]:
                - text: We have implicitly assumed an asynchronous user interface in which a SEND later elicits some kind of SIGNAL or pseudo-interrupt from the serving TCP endpoint. An alternative is to return a response immediately. For instance, SENDs might return immediate local acknowledgment, even if the segment sent had not been acknowledged by the distant TCP endpoint. We could optimistically assume eventual success. If we are wrong, the connection will close anyway due to the timeout. In implementations of this kind (synchronous), there will still be some asynchronous signals, but these will deal with the connection itself, and not with specific segments or buffers.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1871] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.2-14"
              - paragraph [ref=e1872]:
                - text: In order for the process to distinguish among error or success indications for different SENDs, it might be appropriate for the buffer address to be returned along with the coded response to the SEND request. TCP-to-user signals are discussed below, indicating the information that should be returned to the calling process.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1873] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.2-15"
            - generic [ref=e1874]:
              - heading "3.9.1.3. Receive" [level=5] [ref=e1875]:
                - link "3.9.1.3." [ref=e1876] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.3"
                - link "Receive" [ref=e1877] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#name-receive"
              - paragraph [ref=e1878]:
                - text: "Format: RECEIVE (local connection name, buffer address, byte count) -> byte count, URGENT flag [, PUSH flag]"
                - link "¶" [ref=e1879] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.3-1"
              - paragraph [ref=e1880]:
                - text: This command allocates a receiving buffer associated with the specified connection. If no OPEN precedes this command or the calling process is not authorized to use this connection, an error is returned.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1881] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.3-2"
              - paragraph [ref=e1882]:
                - text: In the simplest implementation, control would not return to the calling program until either the buffer was filled or some error occurred, but this scheme is highly subject to deadlocks. A more sophisticated implementation would permit several RECEIVEs to be outstanding at once. These would be filled as segments arrive. This strategy permits increased throughput at the cost of a more elaborate scheme (possibly asynchronous) to notify the calling program that a PUSH has been seen or a buffer filled.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1883] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.3-3"
              - paragraph [ref=e1884]:
                - text: A TCP receiver MAY pass a received PSH bit to the application layer via the PUSH flag in the interface (MAY-17), but it is not required (this was clarified in RFC 1122, Section
                - link "4.2.2.2" [ref=e1885] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc1122#section-4.2.2.2
                - text: ). The remainder of text describing the RECEIVE call below assumes that passing the PUSH indication is supported.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1886] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.3-4"
              - paragraph [ref=e1887]:
                - text: If enough data arrive to fill the buffer before a PUSH is seen, the PUSH flag will not be set in the response to the RECEIVE. The buffer will be filled with as much data as it can hold. If a PUSH is seen before the buffer is filled, the buffer will be returned partially filled and PUSH indicated.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1888] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.3-5"
              - paragraph [ref=e1889]:
                - text: If there is urgent data, the user will have been informed as soon as it arrived via a TCP-to-user signal. The receiving user should thus be in "urgent mode". If the URGENT flag is on, additional urgent data remains. If the URGENT flag is off, this call to RECEIVE has returned all the urgent data, and the user may now leave "urgent mode". Note that data following the urgent pointer (non-urgent data) cannot be delivered to the user in the same buffer with preceding urgent data unless the boundary is clearly marked for the user.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1890] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.3-6"
              - paragraph [ref=e1891]:
                - text: To distinguish among several outstanding RECEIVEs and to take care of the case that a buffer is not completely filled, the return code is accompanied by both a buffer pointer and a byte count indicating the actual length of the data received.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1892] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.3-7"
              - paragraph [ref=e1893]:
                - text: Alternative implementations of RECEIVE might have the TCP endpoint allocate buffer storage, or the TCP endpoint might share a ring buffer with the user.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1894] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.3-8"
            - generic [ref=e1895]:
              - heading "3.9.1.4. Close" [level=5] [ref=e1896]:
                - link "3.9.1.4." [ref=e1897] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.4"
                - link "Close" [ref=e1898] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#name-close"
              - paragraph [ref=e1899]:
                - text: "Format: CLOSE (local connection name)"
                - link "¶" [ref=e1900] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.4-1"
              - paragraph [ref=e1901]:
                - text: This command causes the connection specified to be closed. If the connection is not open or the calling process is not authorized to use this connection, an error is returned. Closing connections is intended to be a graceful operation in the sense that outstanding SENDs will be transmitted (and retransmitted), as flow control permits, until all have been serviced. Thus, it should be acceptable to make several SEND calls, followed by a CLOSE, and expect all the data to be sent to the destination. It should also be clear that users should continue to RECEIVE on CLOSING connections since the remote peer may be trying to transmit the last of its data. Thus, CLOSE means "I have no more to send" but does not mean "I will not receive any more." It may happen (if the user-level protocol is not well thought out) that the closing side is unable to get rid of all its data before timing out. In this event, CLOSE turns into ABORT, and the closing TCP peer gives up.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1902] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.4-2"
              - paragraph [ref=e1903]:
                - text: The user may CLOSE the connection at any time on their own initiative, or in response to various prompts from the TCP implementation (e.g., remote close executed, transmission timeout exceeded, destination inaccessible).
                - link "¶" [ref=e1904] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.4-3"
              - paragraph [ref=e1905]:
                - text: Because closing a connection requires communication with the remote TCP peer, connections may remain in the closing state for a short time. Attempts to reopen the connection before the TCP peer replies to the CLOSE command will result in error responses.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1906] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.4-4"
              - paragraph [ref=e1907]:
                - text: Close also implies push function.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1908] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.4-5"
            - generic [ref=e1909]:
              - heading "3.9.1.5. Status" [level=5] [ref=e1910]:
                - link "3.9.1.5." [ref=e1911] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.5"
                - link "Status" [ref=e1912] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#name-status"
              - paragraph [ref=e1913]:
                - text: "Format: STATUS (local connection name) -> status data"
                - link "¶" [ref=e1914] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.5-1"
              - paragraph [ref=e1915]:
                - text: This is an implementation-dependent user command and could be excluded without adverse effect. Information returned would typically come from the TCB associated with the connection.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1916] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.5-2"
              - paragraph [ref=e1917]:
                - text: "This command returns a data block containing the following information:"
                - link "¶" [ref=e1918] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.5-3"
              - list [ref=e1919]:
                - listitem [ref=e1920]:
                  - text: local socket,
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1921] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.9.1.5-4.1"
                - listitem [ref=e1922]:
                  - text: remote socket,
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1923] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.9.1.5-4.2"
                - listitem [ref=e1924]:
                  - text: local connection name,
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1925] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.9.1.5-4.3"
                - listitem [ref=e1926]:
                  - text: receive window,
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1927] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.9.1.5-4.4"
                - listitem [ref=e1928]:
                  - text: send window,
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1929] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.9.1.5-4.5"
                - listitem [ref=e1930]:
                  - text: connection state,
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1931] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.9.1.5-4.6"
                - listitem [ref=e1932]:
                  - text: number of buffers awaiting acknowledgment,
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1933] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.9.1.5-4.7"
                - listitem [ref=e1934]:
                  - text: number of buffers pending receipt,
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1935] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.9.1.5-4.8"
                - listitem [ref=e1936]:
                  - text: urgent state,
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1937] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.9.1.5-4.9"
                - listitem [ref=e1938]:
                  - text: Diffserv field value,
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1939] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.9.1.5-4.10"
                - listitem [ref=e1940]:
                  - text: security/compartment, and
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1941] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.9.1.5-4.11"
                - listitem [ref=e1942]:
                  - text: transmission timeout.
                  - link "¶" [ref=e1943] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: "#section-3.9.1.5-4.12"
              - paragraph [ref=e1944]:
                - text: Depending on the state of the connection, or on the implementation itself, some of this information may not be available or meaningful. If the calling process is not authorized to use this connection, an error is returned. This prevents unauthorized processes from gaining information about a connection.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1945] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.5-5"
            - generic [ref=e1946]:
              - heading "3.9.1.6. Abort" [level=5] [ref=e1947]:
                - link "3.9.1.6." [ref=e1948] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.6"
                - link "Abort" [ref=e1949] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#name-abort"
              - paragraph [ref=e1950]:
                - text: "Format: ABORT (local connection name)"
                - link "¶" [ref=e1951] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.6-1"
              - paragraph [ref=e1952]:
                - text: This command causes all pending SENDs and RECEIVES to be aborted, the TCB to be removed, and a special RST message to be sent to the remote TCP peer of the connection. Depending on the implementation, users may receive abort indications for each outstanding SEND or RECEIVE, or may simply receive an ABORT-acknowledgment.
                - link "¶" [ref=e1953] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.6-2"
            - generic [ref=e1954]:
              - heading "3.9.1.7. Flush" [level=5] [ref=e1955]:
                - link "3.9.1.7." [ref=e1956] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#section-3.9.1.7"
                - link "Flush" [ref=e1957] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: "#name-flush"
