            - text: "]"
          - text: ", 3602"
          - generic [ref=e3322]:
            - text: "["
            - link "98" [ref=e3323] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid3602
            - text: "]"
          - text: ).
          - link "¶" [ref=e3324] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-5-3"
        - paragraph [ref=e3325]:
          - text: Changes to the specification of the urgent pointer described in RFCs 1011, 1122, and 6093 were incorporated. See RFC 6093 for detailed discussion of why these changes were necessary.
          - link "¶" [ref=e3326] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-5-4"
        - paragraph [ref=e3327]:
          - text: The discussion of the RTO from RFC 793 was updated to refer to RFC 6298. The text on the RTO in RFC 1122 originally replaced the text in RFC 793; however, RFC 2988 should have updated RFC 1122 and has subsequently been obsoleted by RFC 6298.
          - link "¶" [ref=e3328] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-5-5"
        - paragraph [ref=e3329]:
          - text: RFC 1011
          - generic [ref=e3330]:
            - text: "["
            - link "18" [ref=e3331] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1011
            - text: "]"
          - text: contains a number of comments about RFC 793, including some needed changes to the TCP specification. These are expanded in RFC 1122, which contains a collection of other changes and clarifications to RFC 793. The normative items impacting the protocol have been incorporated here, though some historically useful implementation advice and informative discussion from RFC 1122 is not included here. The present document, which is now the TCP specification rather than RFC 793, updates RFC 1011, and the comments noted in RFC 1011 have been incorporated.
          - link "¶" [ref=e3332] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-5-6"
        - paragraph [ref=e3333]:
          - text: RFC 1122 contains more than just TCP requirements, so this document can't obsolete RFC 1122 entirely. It is only marked as "updating" RFC 1122; however, it should be understood to effectively obsolete all of the material on TCP found in RFC 1122.
          - link "¶" [ref=e3334] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-5-7"
        - paragraph [ref=e3335]:
          - text: The more secure initial sequence number generation algorithm from RFC 6528 was incorporated. See RFC 6528 for discussion of the attacks that this mitigates, as well as advice on selecting PRF algorithms and managing secret key data.
          - link "¶" [ref=e3336] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-5-8"
        - paragraph [ref=e3337]:
          - text: A note based on RFC 6429 was added to explicitly clarify that system resource management concerns allow connection resources to be reclaimed. RFC 6429 is obsoleted in the sense that the clarification it describes has been reflected within this base TCP specification.
          - link "¶" [ref=e3338] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-5-9"
        - paragraph [ref=e3339]:
          - text: The description of congestion control implementation was added based on the set of documents that are IETF BCP or Standards Track on the topic and the current state of common implementations.
          - link "¶" [ref=e3340] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-5-10"
      - generic [ref=e3342]:
        - heading "6. IANA Considerations" [level=2] [ref=e3343]:
          - link "6." [ref=e3344] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-6"
          - link "IANA Considerations" [ref=e3345] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#name-iana-considerations"
        - paragraph [ref=e3346]:
          - text: In the "Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) Header Flags" registry, IANA has made several changes as described in this section.
          - link "¶" [ref=e3347] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-6-1"
        - paragraph [ref=e3348]:
          - text: RFC 3168 originally created this registry but only populated it with the new bits defined in RFC 3168, neglecting the other bits that had previously been described in RFC 793 and other documents. Bit 7 has since also been updated by RFC 8311
          - generic [ref=e3349]:
            - text: "["
            - link "54" [ref=e3350] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8311
            - text: "]"
          - text: .
          - link "¶" [ref=e3351] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-6-2"
        - paragraph [ref=e3352]:
          - text: The "Bit" column has been renamed below as the "Bit Offset" column because it references each header flag's offset within the 16-bit aligned view of the TCP header in
          - link "Figure 1" [ref=e3353] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#header_format"
          - text: . The bits in offsets 0 through 3 are the TCP segment Data Offset field, and not header flags.
          - link "¶" [ref=e3354] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-6-3"
        - paragraph [ref=e3355]:
          - text: IANA has added a column for "Assignment Notes".
          - link "¶" [ref=e3356] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-6-4"
        - paragraph [ref=e3357]:
          - text: IANA has assigned values as indicated below.
          - link "¶" [ref=e3358] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-6-5"
        - 'table "Table 7: TCP Header Flags" [ref=e3359]':
          - caption [ref=e3360]:
            - link "Table 7" [ref=e3361] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#table-7"
            - text: ":"
            - link "TCP Header Flags" [ref=e3362] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#name-tcp-header-flags"
          - rowgroup [ref=e3363]:
            - row "Bit Offset Name Reference Assignment Notes" [ref=e3364]:
              - columnheader "Bit Offset" [ref=e3365]
              - columnheader "Name" [ref=e3366]
              - columnheader "Reference" [ref=e3367]
              - columnheader "Assignment Notes" [ref=e3368]
          - rowgroup [ref=e3369]:
            - row "4 Reserved for future use RFC 9293" [ref=e3370]:
              - cell "4" [ref=e3371]
              - cell "Reserved for future use" [ref=e3372]
              - cell "RFC 9293" [ref=e3373]
              - cell [ref=e3374]
            - row "5 Reserved for future use RFC 9293" [ref=e3375]:
              - cell "5" [ref=e3376]
              - cell "Reserved for future use" [ref=e3377]
              - cell "RFC 9293" [ref=e3378]
              - cell [ref=e3379]
            - row "6 Reserved for future use RFC 9293" [ref=e3380]:
              - cell "6" [ref=e3381]
              - cell "Reserved for future use" [ref=e3382]
              - cell "RFC 9293" [ref=e3383]
              - cell [ref=e3384]
            - row "7 Reserved for future use RFC 8311 Previously used by Historic RFC 3540 as NS (Nonce Sum)." [ref=e3385]:
              - cell "7" [ref=e3386]
              - cell "Reserved for future use" [ref=e3387]
              - cell "RFC 8311" [ref=e3388]
              - cell "Previously used by Historic RFC 3540 as NS (Nonce Sum)." [ref=e3389]
            - row "8 CWR (Congestion Window Reduced) RFC 3168" [ref=e3390]:
              - cell "8" [ref=e3391]
              - cell "CWR (Congestion Window Reduced)" [ref=e3392]
              - cell "RFC 3168" [ref=e3393]
              - cell [ref=e3394]
            - row "9 ECE (ECN-Echo) RFC 3168" [ref=e3395]:
              - cell "9" [ref=e3396]
              - cell "ECE (ECN-Echo)" [ref=e3397]
              - cell "RFC 3168" [ref=e3398]
              - cell [ref=e3399]
            - row "10 Urgent pointer field is significant (URG) RFC 9293" [ref=e3400]:
              - cell "10" [ref=e3401]
              - cell "Urgent pointer field is significant (URG)" [ref=e3402]
              - cell "RFC 9293" [ref=e3403]
              - cell [ref=e3404]
            - row "11 Acknowledgment field is significant (ACK) RFC 9293" [ref=e3405]:
              - cell "11" [ref=e3406]
              - cell "Acknowledgment field is significant (ACK)" [ref=e3407]
              - cell "RFC 9293" [ref=e3408]
              - cell [ref=e3409]
            - row "12 Push function (PSH) RFC 9293" [ref=e3410]:
              - cell "12" [ref=e3411]
              - cell "Push function (PSH)" [ref=e3412]
              - cell "RFC 9293" [ref=e3413]
              - cell [ref=e3414]
            - row "13 Reset the connection (RST) RFC 9293" [ref=e3415]:
              - cell "13" [ref=e3416]
              - cell "Reset the connection (RST)" [ref=e3417]
              - cell "RFC 9293" [ref=e3418]
              - cell [ref=e3419]
            - row "14 Synchronize sequence numbers (SYN) RFC 9293" [ref=e3420]:
              - cell "14" [ref=e3421]
              - cell "Synchronize sequence numbers (SYN)" [ref=e3422]
              - cell "RFC 9293" [ref=e3423]
              - cell [ref=e3424]
            - row "15 No more data from sender (FIN) RFC 9293" [ref=e3425]:
              - cell "15" [ref=e3426]
              - cell "No more data from sender (FIN)" [ref=e3427]
              - cell "RFC 9293" [ref=e3428]
              - cell [ref=e3429]
        - paragraph [ref=e3430]:
          - text: The "TCP Header Flags" registry has also been moved to a subregistry under the global "Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) Parameters" registry
          - generic [ref=e3431]:
            - text: <
            - link "https://www.iana.org/assignments/tcp-parameters/" [ref=e3432] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://www.iana.org/assignments/tcp-parameters/
            - text: ">"
          - text: .
          - link "¶" [ref=e3433] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-6-7"
        - paragraph [ref=e3434]:
          - text: The registry's Registration Procedure remains Standards Action, but the Reference has been updated to this document, and the Note has been removed.
          - link "¶" [ref=e3435] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-6-8"
      - generic [ref=e3437]:
        - heading "7. Security and Privacy Considerations" [level=2] [ref=e3438]:
          - link "7." [ref=e3439] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-7"
          - link "Security and Privacy Considerations" [ref=e3440] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#name-security-and-privacy-consid"
        - paragraph [ref=e3441]:
          - text: The TCP design includes only rudimentary security features that improve the robustness and reliability of connections and application data transfer, but there are no built-in cryptographic capabilities to support any form of confidentiality, authentication, or other typical security functions. Non-cryptographic enhancements (e.g.,
          - generic [ref=e3442]:
            - text: "["
            - link "9" [ref=e3443] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc5961
            - text: "]"
          - text: ) have been developed to improve robustness of TCP connections to particular types of attacks, but the applicability and protections of non-cryptographic enhancements are limited (e.g., see
          - generic [ref=e3444]:
            - link "Section 1.1" [ref=e3445] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5961#section-1.1
            - text: of [
            - link "9" [ref=e3446] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc5961
            - text: "]"
          - text: ). Applications typically utilize lower-layer (e.g., IPsec) and upper-layer (e.g., TLS) protocols to provide security and privacy for TCP connections and application data carried in TCP. Methods based on TCP Options have been developed as well, to support some security capabilities.
          - link "¶" [ref=e3447] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-7-1"
        - paragraph [ref=e3448]:
          - text: In order to fully provide confidentiality, integrity protection, and authentication for TCP connections (including their control flags), IPsec is the only current effective method. For integrity protection and authentication, the TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO)
          - generic [ref=e3449]:
            - text: "["
            - link "38" [ref=e3450] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc5925
            - text: "]"
          - text: is available, with a proposed extension to also provide confidentiality for the segment payload. Other methods discussed in this section may provide confidentiality or integrity protection for the payload, but for the TCP header only cover either a subset of the fields (e.g., tcpcrypt
          - generic [ref=e3451]:
            - text: "["
            - link "57" [ref=e3452] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8548
            - text: "]"
          - text: ) or none at all (e.g., TLS). Other security features that have been added to TCP (e.g., ISN generation, sequence number checks, and others) are only capable of partially hindering attacks.
          - link "¶" [ref=e3453] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-7-2"
        - paragraph [ref=e3454]:
          - text: Applications using long-lived TCP flows have been vulnerable to attacks that exploit the processing of control flags described in earlier TCP specifications
          - generic [ref=e3455]:
            - text: "["
            - link "33" [ref=e3456] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc4953
            - text: "]"
          - text: . TCP-MD5 was a commonly implemented TCP Option to support authentication for some of these connections, but had flaws and is now deprecated. TCP-AO provides a capability to protect long-lived TCP connections from attacks and has superior properties to TCP-MD5. It does not provide any privacy for application data or for the TCP headers.
          - link "¶" [ref=e3457] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-7-3"
        - paragraph [ref=e3458]:
          - text: The "tcpcrypt"
          - generic [ref=e3459]:
            - text: "["
            - link "57" [ref=e3460] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8548
            - text: "]"
          - text: experimental extension to TCP provides the ability to cryptographically protect connection data. Metadata aspects of the TCP flow are still visible, but the application stream is well protected. Within the TCP header, only the urgent pointer and FIN flag are protected through tcpcrypt.
          - link "¶" [ref=e3461] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-7-4"
        - paragraph [ref=e3462]:
          - text: The TCP Roadmap
          - generic [ref=e3463]:
            - text: "["
            - link "49" [ref=e3464] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7414
            - text: "]"
          - text: includes notes about several RFCs related to TCP security. Many of the enhancements provided by these RFCs have been integrated into the present document, including ISN generation, mitigating blind in-window attacks, and improving handling of soft errors and ICMP packets. These are all discussed in greater detail in the referenced RFCs that originally described the changes needed to earlier TCP specifications. Additionally, see RFC 6093
          - generic [ref=e3465]:
            - text: "["
            - link "39" [ref=e3466] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6093
            - text: "]"
          - text: for discussion of security considerations related to the urgent pointer field, which also discourages new applications from using the urgent pointer.
          - link "¶" [ref=e3467] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-7-5"
        - paragraph [ref=e3468]:
          - text: Since TCP is often used for bulk transfer flows, some attacks are possible that abuse the TCP congestion control logic. An example is "ACK-division" attacks. Updates that have been made to the TCP congestion control specifications include mechanisms like Appropriate Byte Counting (ABC)
          - generic [ref=e3469]:
            - text: "["
            - link "29" [ref=e3470] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc3465
            - text: "]"
          - text: that act as mitigations to these attacks.
          - link "¶" [ref=e3471] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-7-6"
        - paragraph [ref=e3472]:
          - text: Other attacks are focused on exhausting the resources of a TCP server. Examples include SYN flooding
          - generic [ref=e3473]:
            - text: "["
            - link "32" [ref=e3474] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc4987
            - text: "]"
          - text: or wasting resources on non-progressing connections
          - generic [ref=e3475]:
            - text: "["
            - link "41" [ref=e3476] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6429
            - text: "]"
          - text: . Operating systems commonly implement mitigations for these attacks. Some common defenses also utilize proxies, stateful firewalls, and other technologies outside the end-host TCP implementation.
          - link "¶" [ref=e3477] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-7-7"
        - paragraph [ref=e3478]:
          - text: The concept of a protocol's "wire image" is described in RFC 8546
          - generic [ref=e3479]:
            - text: "["
            - link "56" [ref=e3480] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8546
            - text: "]"
          - text: ", which describes how TCP's cleartext headers expose more metadata to nodes on the path than is strictly required to route the packets to their destination. On-path adversaries may be able to leverage this metadata. Lessons learned in this respect from TCP have been applied in the design of newer transports like QUIC"
          - generic [ref=e3481]:
            - text: "["
            - link "60" [ref=e3482] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc9000
            - text: "]"
          - text: . Additionally, based partly on experiences with TCP and its extensions, there are considerations that might be applicable for future TCP extensions and other transports that the IETF has documented in RFC 9065
          - generic [ref=e3483]:
            - text: "["
            - link "61" [ref=e3484] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc9065
            - text: "]"
          - text: ", along with IAB recommendations in RFC 8558"
          - generic [ref=e3485]:
            - text: "["
            - link "58" [ref=e3486] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8558
            - text: "]"
          - text: and
          - generic [ref=e3487]:
            - text: "["
            - link "67" [ref=e3488] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc9170
            - text: "]"
          - text: .
          - link "¶" [ref=e3489] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-7-8"
        - paragraph [ref=e3490]:
          - text: There are also methods of "fingerprinting" that can be used to infer the host TCP implementation (operating system) version or platform information. These collect observations of several aspects, such as the options present in segments, the ordering of options, the specific behaviors in the case of various conditions, packet timing, packet sizing, and other aspects of the protocol that are left to be determined by an implementer, and can use those observations to identify information about the host and implementation.
          - link "¶" [ref=e3491] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-7-9"
        - paragraph [ref=e3492]:
          - text: Since ICMP message processing also can interact with TCP connections, there is potential for ICMP-based attacks against TCP connections. These are discussed in RFC 5927
          - generic [ref=e3493]:
            - text: "["
            - link "100" [ref=e3494] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc5927
            - text: "]"
          - text: ", along with mitigations that have been implemented."
          - link "¶" [ref=e3495] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#section-7-10"
      - generic [ref=e3496]:
        - heading "8. References" [level=2] [ref=e3497]:

            - term [ref=e3872]: "[74]"

              - text: RFC Errata, Erratum ID 574, RFC 793,
              - generic [ref=e3874]:
                - text: <
                - link "https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid574" [ref=e3875] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid574
                - text: ">"
              - text: .
            - term [ref=e3876]: "[75]"
            - definition [ref=e3877]:
              - text: RFC Errata, Erratum ID 700, RFC 793,
              - generic [ref=e3878]:
                - text: <
                - link "https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid700" [ref=e3879] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid700
                - text: ">"
              - text: .
            - term [ref=e3880]: "[76]"
            - definition [ref=e3881]:
              - text: RFC Errata, Erratum ID 701, RFC 793,
              - generic [ref=e3882]:
                - text: <
                - link "https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid701" [ref=e3883] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid701
                - text: ">"
              - text: .
            - term [ref=e3884]: "[77]"
            - definition [ref=e3885]:
              - text: RFC Errata, Erratum ID 1283, RFC 793,
              - generic [ref=e3886]:
                - text: <
                - link "https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid1283" [ref=e3887] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid1283

              - text: .

              - generic [ref=e3890]:

            - definition [ref=e3949]:

              - generic [ref=e3950]:
                - text: <

                  - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid575

              - generic [ref=e3954]:
                - text: <

                  - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid2296

            - definition [ref=e3965]:
              - text: RFC Errata, Erratum ID 3305, RFC 793,
              - generic [ref=e3966]:
                - text: <
                - link "https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid3305" [ref=e3967] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid3305

                  - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid3602

                  - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid4772

            - term [ref=e3976]: "[100]"
            - definition [ref=e3977]:
              - text: Gont, F., "ICMP Attacks against TCP", RFC 5927, DOI 10.17487/RFC5927,
              - time [ref=e3978]: July 2010
              - text: ","
              - generic [ref=e3979]:
                - text: <
                - link "https://www.rfc-editor.org/info/rfc5927" [ref=e3980] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/info/rfc5927
                - text: ">"
              - text: .
      - generic [ref=e3981]:
        - heading "Appendix A. Other Implementation Notes" [level=2] [ref=e3982]:
          - link "Appendix A." [ref=e3983] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#appendix-A"
          - link "Other Implementation Notes" [ref=e3984] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#name-other-implementation-notes"
        - paragraph [ref=e3985]:
          - text: This section includes additional notes and references on TCP implementation decisions that are currently not a part of the RFC series or included within the TCP standard. These items can be considered by implementers, but there was not yet a consensus to include them in the standard.
          - link "¶" [ref=e3986] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#appendix-A-1"
        - generic [ref=e3988]:
          - heading "A.1. IP Security Compartment and Precedence" [level=3] [ref=e3989]:
            - link "A.1." [ref=e3990] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#appendix-A.1"
            - link "IP Security Compartment and Precedence" [ref=e3991] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#name-ip-security-compartment-and"
          - paragraph [ref=e3992]:
            - text: The IPv4 specification
            - generic [ref=e3993]:
              - text: "["
              - link "1" [ref=e3994] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc791
              - text: "]"
            - text: includes a precedence value in the (now obsoleted) Type of Service (TOS) field. It was modified in
            - generic [ref=e3995]:
              - text: "["
              - link "20" [ref=e3996] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1349
              - text: "]"
            - text: and then obsoleted by the definition of Differentiated Services (Diffserv)
            - generic [ref=e3997]:
              - text: "["
              - link "4" [ref=e3998] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc2474
              - text: "]"
            - text: . Setting and conveying TOS between the network layer, TCP implementation, and applications is obsolete and is replaced by Diffserv in the current TCP specification.
            - link "¶" [ref=e3999] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#appendix-A.1-1"
          - paragraph [ref=e4000]:
            - text: RFC 793 required checking the IP security compartment and precedence on incoming TCP segments for consistency within a connection and with application requests. Each of these aspects of IP have become outdated, without specific updates to RFC 793. The issues with precedence were fixed by
            - generic [ref=e4001]:
              - text: "["
              - link "25" [ref=e4002] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc2873
              - text: "]"
            - text: ", which is Standards Track, and so this present TCP specification includes those changes. However, the state of IP security options that may be used by Multi-Level Secure (MLS) systems is not as apparent in the IETF currently."
            - link "¶" [ref=e4003] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#appendix-A.1-2"
          - paragraph [ref=e4004]:
            - text: Resetting connections when incoming packets do not meet expected security compartment or precedence expectations has been recognized as a possible attack vector
            - generic [ref=e4005]:
              - text: "["
              - link "63" [ref=e4006] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-gont-tcpm-tcp-seccomp-prec-00
              - text: "]"
            - text: ", and there has been discussion about amending the TCP specification to prevent connections from being aborted due to nonmatching IP security compartment and Diffserv codepoint values."
            - link "¶" [ref=e4007] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#appendix-A.1-3"
          - generic [ref=e4008]:
            - heading "A.1.1. Precedence" [level=4] [ref=e4009]:
              - link "A.1.1." [ref=e4010] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#appendix-A.1.1"
              - link "Precedence" [ref=e4011] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#name-precedence"
            - paragraph [ref=e4012]:
              - text: In Diffserv, the former precedence values are treated as Class Selector codepoints, and methods for compatible treatment are described in the Diffserv architecture. The RFC TCP specification defined by RFCs 793 and 1122 included logic intending to have connections use the highest precedence requested by either endpoint application, and to keep the precedence consistent throughout a connection. This logic from the obsolete TOS is not applicable to Diffserv and should not be included in TCP implementations, though changes to Diffserv values within a connection are discouraged. For discussion of this, see RFC 7657 (Sections
              - link "5.1" [ref=e4013] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7657#section-5.1
              - text: ","
              - link "5.3" [ref=e4014] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7657#section-5.3
              - text: ", and"
              - link "6" [ref=e4015] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7657#section-6
              - text: )
              - generic [ref=e4016]:
                - text: "["
                - link "50" [ref=e4017] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7657
                - text: "]"
              - text: .
              - link "¶" [ref=e4018] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#appendix-A.1.1-1"
            - paragraph [ref=e4019]:
              - text: The obsoleted TOS processing rules in TCP assumed bidirectional (or symmetric) precedence values used on a connection, but the Diffserv architecture is asymmetric. Problems with the old TCP logic in this regard were described in
              - generic [ref=e4020]:
                - text: "["
                - link "25" [ref=e4021] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc2873
                - text: "]"
              - text: ", and the solution described is to ignore IP precedence in TCP. Since RFC 2873 is a Standards Track document (although not marked as updating RFC 793), current implementations are expected to be robust in these conditions. Note that the Diffserv field value used in each direction is a part of the interface between TCP and the network layer, and values in use can be indicated both ways between TCP and the application."
              - link "¶" [ref=e4022] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#appendix-A.1.1-2"
          - generic [ref=e4023]:
            - heading "A.1.2. MLS Systems" [level=4] [ref=e4024]:
              - link "A.1.2." [ref=e4025] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#appendix-A.1.2"
              - link "MLS Systems" [ref=e4026] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#name-mls-systems"
            - paragraph [ref=e4027]:
              - text: The IP Security Option (IPSO) and compartment defined in
              - generic [ref=e4028]:
                - text: "["
                - link "1" [ref=e4029] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc791
                - text: "]"
              - text: was refined in RFC 1038, which was later obsoleted by RFC 1108. The Commercial IP Security Option (CIPSO) is defined in FIPS-188 (withdrawn by NIST in 2015) and is supported by some vendors and operating systems. RFC 1108 is now Historic, though RFC 791 itself has not been updated to remove the IP Security Option. For IPv6, a similar option (Common Architecture Label IPv6 Security Option (CALIPSO)) has been defined
              - generic [ref=e4030]:
                - text: "["
                - link "36" [ref=e4031] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc5570
                - text: "]"
              - text: . RFC 793 includes logic that includes the IP security/compartment information in treatment of TCP segments. References to the IP "security/compartment" in this document may be relevant for Multi-Level Secure (MLS) system implementers but can be ignored for non-MLS implementations, consistent with running code on the Internet. See
              - link "Appendix A.1" [ref=e4032] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#seccomp"
              - text: for further discussion. Note that RFC 5570 describes some MLS networking scenarios where IPSO, CIPSO, or CALIPSO may be used. In these special cases, TCP implementers should see Section
              - link "7.3.1" [ref=e4033] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5570#section-7.3.1
              - text: of RFC 5570 and follow the guidance in that document.
              - link "¶" [ref=e4034] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#appendix-A.1.2-1"
        - generic [ref=e4036]:
          - heading "A.2. Sequence Number Validation" [level=3] [ref=e4037]:
            - link "A.2." [ref=e4038] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#appendix-A.2"
            - link "Sequence Number Validation" [ref=e4039] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#name-sequence-number-validation"
          - paragraph [ref=e4040]:
            - text: There are cases where the TCP sequence number validation rules can prevent ACK fields from being processed. This can result in connection issues, as described in
            - generic [ref=e4041]:
              - text: "["
              - link "64" [ref=e4042] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-gont-tcpm-tcp-seq-validation-04
              - text: "]"
            - text: ", which includes descriptions of potential problems in conditions of simultaneous open, self-connects, simultaneous close, and simultaneous window probes. The document also describes potential changes to the TCP specification to mitigate the issue by expanding the acceptable sequence numbers."
            - link "¶" [ref=e4043] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#appendix-A.2-1"
          - paragraph [ref=e4044]:
            - text: In Internet usage of TCP, these conditions rarely occur. Common operating systems include different alternative mitigations, and the standard has not been updated yet to codify one of them, but implementers should consider the problems described in
            - generic [ref=e4045]:
              - text: "["
              - link "64" [ref=e4046] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-gont-tcpm-tcp-seq-validation-04
              - text: "]"
            - text: .
            - link "¶" [ref=e4047] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#appendix-A.2-2"
        - generic [ref=e4049]:
          - heading "A.3. Nagle Modification" [level=3] [ref=e4050]:
            - link "A.3." [ref=e4051] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#appendix-A.3"
            - link "Nagle Modification" [ref=e4052] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#name-nagle-modification"
          - paragraph [ref=e4053]:
            - text: In common operating systems, both the Nagle algorithm and delayed acknowledgments are implemented and enabled by default. TCP is used by many applications that have a request-response style of communication, where the combination of the Nagle algorithm and delayed acknowledgments can result in poor application performance. A modification to the Nagle algorithm is described in
            - generic [ref=e4054]:
              - text: "["
              - link "68" [ref=e4055] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-minshall-nagle-01
              - text: "]"
            - text: that improves the situation for these applications.
            - link "¶" [ref=e4056] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#appendix-A.3-1"
          - paragraph [ref=e4057]:
            - text: This modification is implemented in some common operating systems and does not impact TCP interoperability. Additionally, many applications simply disable Nagle since this is generally supported by a socket option. The TCP standard has not been updated to include this Nagle modification, but implementers may find it beneficial to consider.
            - link "¶" [ref=e4058] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#appendix-A.3-2"
        - generic [ref=e4059]:
          - heading "A.4. Low Watermark Settings" [level=3] [ref=e4060]:
            - link "A.4." [ref=e4061] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#appendix-A.4"
            - link "Low Watermark Settings" [ref=e4062] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#name-low-watermark-settings"
          - paragraph [ref=e4063]:
            - text: Some operating system kernel TCP implementations include socket options that allow specifying the number of bytes in the buffer until the socket layer will pass sent data to TCP (SO_SNDLOWAT) or to the application on receiving (SO_RCVLOWAT).
            - link "¶" [ref=e4064] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#appendix-A.4-1"
          - paragraph [ref=e4065]:
            - text: In addition, another socket option (TCP_NOTSENT_LOWAT) can be used to control the amount of unsent bytes in the write queue. This can help a sending TCP application to avoid creating large amounts of buffered data (and corresponding latency). As an example, this may be useful for applications that are multiplexing data from multiple upper-level streams onto a connection, especially when streams may be a mix of interactive/real-time and bulk data transfer.
            - link "¶" [ref=e4066] [cursor=pointer]:
              - /url: "#appendix-A.4-2"
      - generic [ref=e4068]:
        - heading "Appendix B. TCP Requirement Summary" [level=2] [ref=e4069]:
          - link "Appendix B." [ref=e4070] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#appendix-B"
          - link "TCP Requirement Summary" [ref=e4071] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#name-tcp-requirement-summary"
        - paragraph [ref=e4072]:
          - text: This section is adapted from RFC 1122.
          - link "¶" [ref=e4073] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#appendix-B-1"
        - paragraph [ref=e4074]:
          - text: Note that there is no requirement related to PLPMTUD in this list, but that PLPMTUD is recommended.
          - link "¶" [ref=e4075] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#appendix-B-2"
        - 'table "Table 8: TCP Requirements Summary" [ref=e4077]':
          - caption [ref=e4078]:

      - generic [ref=e5256]:
        - heading "Acknowledgments" [level=2] [ref=e5257]:
          - link "Acknowledgments" [ref=e5258] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#name-acknowledgments"
        - paragraph [ref=e5259]:
          - text: This document is largely a revision of RFC 793, of which Jon Postel was the editor. Due to his excellent work, it was able to last for three decades before we felt the need to revise it.
          - link "¶" [ref=e5260] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#appendix-C-1"
        - paragraph [ref=e5261]:
          - text: Andre Oppermann was a contributor and helped to edit the first revision of this document.
          - link "¶" [ref=e5262] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#appendix-C-2"
        - paragraph [ref=e5263]:
          - text: "We are thankful for the assistance of the IETF TCPM working group chairs over the course of work on this document:"
          - link "¶" [ref=e5264] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#appendix-C-3"
        - generic [ref=e5266]: Michael Scharf
        - generic [ref=e5268]: Yoshifumi Nishida
        - generic [ref=e5270]: Pasi Sarolahti
        - generic [ref=e5272]: Michael Tüxen
        - paragraph [ref=e5273]:
          - text: "During the discussions of this work on the TCPM mailing list, in working group meetings, and via area reviews, helpful comments, critiques, and reviews were received from (listed alphabetically by last name): Praveen Balasubramanian, David Borman, Mohamed Boucadair, Bob Briscoe, Neal Cardwell, Yuchung Cheng, Martin Duke, Francis Dupont, Ted Faber, Gorry Fairhurst, Fernando Gont, Rodney Grimes, Yi Huang, Rahul Jadhav, Markku Kojo, Mike Kosek, Juhamatti Kuusisaari, Kevin Lahey, Kevin Mason, Matt Mathis, Stephen McQuistin, Jonathan Morton, Matt Olson, Tommy Pauly, Tom Petch, Hagen Paul Pfeifer, Kyle Rose, Anthony Sabatini, Michael Scharf, Greg Skinner, Joe Touch, Michael Tüxen, Reji Varghese, Bernie Volz, Tim Wicinski, Lloyd Wood, and Alex Zimmermann."
          - link "¶" [ref=e5274] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#appendix-C-4"
        - paragraph [ref=e5275]:
          - text: Joe Touch provided additional help in clarifying the description of segment size parameters and PMTUD/PLPMTUD recommendations. Markku Kojo helped put together the text in the section on TCP Congestion Control.
          - link "¶" [ref=e5276] [cursor=pointer]:
            - /url: "#appendix-C-5"
        - paragraph [ref=e5277]:
          - text: "This document includes content from errata that were reported by (listed chronologically): Yin Shuming, Bob Braden, Morris M. Keesan, Pei-chun Cheng, Constantin Hagemeier, Vishwas Manral, Mykyta Yevstifeyev, EungJun Yi, Botong Huang, Charles Deng, Merlin Buge."

            - /url: "#appendix-C-6"

                    - listitem [ref=e5389]:
                      - link "RFC 9293" [ref=e5390] [cursor=pointer]:
                        - /url: /doc/html/rfc9293
